articles.full_text:
Ground ReportTraditional cooling ideas tackle Rajasthan's extreme heat:Rooftop farming, eco-friendly shelters provide nature-based solutions
- Copy link
Temperatures in several cities have crossed even 45°C in Rajasthan's summer. It is difficult to survive without air conditioners in homes and offices. However, there are many people who are reducing the temperature of homes and offices by adopting traditional models.
On one hand, there is a model included in the net-zero cooling stations built in Jaipur, where people are getting relief without air conditioners. On the other hand, there is the rooftop farming model of Jaipur entrepreneur Pratik Tiwari.
This has emerged from Rajasthan and reached more than 32 cities across the country and is converting the scorching roofs of thousands of homes into natural air conditioners.
In Dainik Bhaskar's World Environment Day special ground report, an investigation of both these models, which are not only providing relief from heat, but are also showing that the way to keep future cities cool perhaps lies more in nature than in machines.
Model-1: Okra, Eggplant, and Pumpkin growing on the roof
The rooftop of Mukesh Vaswani's house in Mansarovar, Jaipur, is no less than a farm scene. When the team reached his rooftop, large grow bags and containers were filled with flourishing okra, eggplant, pumpkin, tomatoes, chillies, and various green vegetables.
Despite the intense heat, the greenery on the rooftop made the temperature feel relatively cooler. This was not just an experiment in growing vegetables, but a model to combat rising heat that was also helping to naturally cool the house.
Mukesh Vaswani explains that until a year ago, his rooftop would also heat up during the summer. It would become difficult to stand on the roof during the afternoon hours, and the rooms in the house would remain hot until late at night.
But after starting rooftop farming, the situation has changed. Now with a layer of soil, plants, and vegetables on the roof, sunlight doesn't fall directly on the concrete, and the temperature inside the house remains lower compared to before.
Left corporate job, built a startup
Behind this transformation is the model of Jaipur entrepreneur Prateek Tiwari. Prateek explains that he, too, started from his home. The increasing heat and constantly rising electricity bills had forced him to think.
He started organic farming on his rooftop and, within a few months, realized that the house was staying cooler than before. After this, he transformed this idea into a mission.
Leaving his corporate job, he started his startup named 'Living Greens Organics' in 2017. He began providing people with solutions to naturally cool homes through rooftop farming. Today, more than 4,000 families in over 32 cities across the country are connected with his model.
When the roof became a 'Natural Air Conditioner'
Pratik explains that rooftop farming is not just a means of growing vegetables, but it also works like a natural cooling system. The soil and plants present on the roof absorb a large portion of the sun's heat, which keeps the concrete less hot and maintains the temperature in the rooms below.
According to him, the need for ACs and coolers has reduced in many homes, which has also led to a decrease in electricity bills. Additionally, families are getting fresh and chemical-free vegetables.
Plants became children's friends instead of mobiles
Manasarovar resident Bhavna says that after starting rooftop farming, the atmosphere of her home has completely changed. Compost is prepared from organic waste generated at home, which is used for plants.
Children also get an opportunity to take care of plants and stay close to nature. She says that earlier, children used to remain glued to mobiles in their free time, but now they take an interest in watering plants and growing vegetables.
70% government subsidy has increased people's interest
To promote rooftop farming, the Agriculture Department launched the 'Smart City Rooftop Farming Project' in Jaipur last year. Under the pilot project, 250 houses in the municipal corporation area were selected. This selection was done on a 'first-come, first-served' basis, and only residential buildings were included.
The responsibility of the project was given to the International Society for Horticultural Innovation and Training Centre (ISITC) located in Durgapura. The government is providing a subsidy of up to 70 percent on the cost of rooftop farming.
Model-2: Bus stand getting cool without AC
The Net Zero Cooling Station built at the bus stand located on VT Road in Mansarovar appears to be an ordinary shed at first glance, but as soon as someone enters inside, the temperature difference between outside and inside becomes immediately noticeable.
Built through a partnership between Mahila Housing Trust (MHT), Jaipur Municipal Corporation, and IIFL Foundation, this station does not have an air conditioner installed. Despite this, the temperature here feels 5 to 6 degrees lower compared to outside.
The secret lies in khus curtains, water sprays, solar power, and traditional ventilation techniques. Water is continuously poured on the khus curtains installed on the station walls. When hot air passes through these wet curtains, it cools down and enters inside.
The wind tower built on top expels the hot air out, which maintains a continuous flow of cool air. The entire system is powered by solar energy.
When the Dainik Bhaskar team reached the spot, laborers, delivery boys, and passersby were seen taking relief from the heat. Laborer Ramavatar says, ‘We work in the sun from morning to evening. Sometimes the condition becomes bad. Sitting here for a while gives relief to the body. It feels like getting a short break from the heat.’
Harkesh, who is a daily wage laborer, says that such centers should be there throughout the city. People like us who stay in the sun all day need them the most. According to center staff Anjali, more than 100 people come here daily. These include laborers, auto drivers, delivery agents, parcel workers, and passersby.
This model is important for this reason, too. Because it brings back the same technique of Rajasthan's traditional havelis and stepwells in a modern form, where temperature was controlled through water, air, and shade.
Rachna Sharma, Program Coordinator of Women's Housing Trust, said that it costs approximately ₹12 to 13 lakh to establish the Net Cooling Center in Jaipur. These have been installed at a total of four places in Rajasthan.
One in Jaipur, two in Jodhpur, and one in Churu. We are also making efforts in other cities of Rajasthan. This was our pilot project, which has received a lot of appreciation.
